video_title: Why the Last Short Seller Is Preparing Now | Carson Block Explains
gist: Muddy Waters' Carson Block argues activist short selling has become structurally unscalable and politically hostile, pushing his firm to diversify into systematic S&P 500 momentum trading and junior mining. He lays out a detailed accounting-based short thesis on SoFi, a capped-risk 'AI disruption' trade wagering that AI could displace roughly 15% of US knowledge workers and unwind 401(k)-driven flows into mega-cap stocks, and reiterates that China remains uninvestable due to poor information quality and policy capriciousness.

insights.claim: Muddy Waters' activist short-selling franchise is structurally capped at only a handful of new campaigns per year, because a target has to be badly broken and misunderstood enough to be worth researching and publishing on, which limits deployable capital regardless of how much AUM is offered.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest (Muddy Waters founder)
insights.so_what: Explains why capital can't scale into this strategy and why so few activist short sellers remain in business today.
insights.quote: The dirty secret of the activist short-selling model is that it's not scalable.

insights.claim: Passive short selling -- taking short positions without publishing research to move the market -- has been a horrific business model for the industry since the 2008 financial crisis because identifying risk hasn't been rewarded by markets that keep getting rescued.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Signals why surviving short sellers must be activist/research-driven, and why outright short interest has generally been a losing strategy for over a decade.
insights.quote: if you want to be shorting equities without publishing on them, I mean, that's just been a horrific business model post-GFC.

insights.claim: The social climate around short selling has turned hostile: retail-heavy shareholder bases like SoFi's now cheer managements that aggressively retaliate against critics, including tolerating death threats against short sellers on social media without pushback.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Higher personal and reputational risk raises the real-world cost of activist short campaigns, a factor in why the profession is shrinking.
insights.quote: there were some like pretty nasty death threats out there, and and like nobody even, you know, on Twitter, and nobody even said, you know, "Hey, that's too far."

insights.claim: Short sellers show a higher rate of depression and mental illness than other professional investors according to Jim Chanos, a dynamic that pushed Block to become more pragmatic (comparing himself to rival short seller Andrew Left) and to diversify Muddy Waters beyond pure short activism into momentum and mining investing.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Explains the strategic pivot of a top short-selling firm toward long-side and systematic strategies, another signal the short-selling niche is contracting even among survivors.
insights.quote: it's been shown that there's a higher rate of depression and mental illness among short sellers than among other professional investors.

insights.claim: Carson Block believes AI could displace roughly 15% of US knowledge workers within three years, concentrated among high earners including junior lawyers, accountants, and coders.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A displacement shock concentrated among high earners would hit household income and asset-buying power broadly, not just blue-collar sectors -- watch layoff data in professional services.
insights.quote: we think 15% you know, roughly 15% of US knowledge workers within 3 years is possible.

insights.claim: Unlike the 2008 financial crisis, Block argues AI-driven layoffs won't be followed by 'green shoots' -- replacement jobs -- because there isn't enough new job creation to reabsorb displaced high-earning knowledge workers at comparable pay.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Implies a structural, not cyclical, hit to labor income and consumption if the displacement thesis plays out.
insights.quote: it's not like the GFC where you have green shoots, as they used to call it, on the other side.

insights.claim: Because 401(k) contribution flows have been a key structural driver powering the S&P 500's largest stocks, Block expects that as displaced knowledge workers lose income those flows will go neutral and then negative -- compounded by boomers drawing down retirement assets -- causing a hard unwind in the mega-cap names that most benefited from those flows.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete mechanism linking AI-driven job losses to a specific market outcome (mega-cap unwind) investors could monitor via 401(k) flow and mega-cap breadth data.
insights.quote: the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 in particular, the largest stocks, the ones that have been the biggest beneficiaries of these flywheels of flows, those will unwind hard.

insights.claim: Block argues this wave of AI disruption differs from past technology shifts because AI models are increasingly used to code and test their own successors, producing exponential rather than step-change capability gains that humans won't be able to keep pace with.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Suggests the labor-market adjustment window could be much shorter than past technology transitions, compressing the disruption timeline.
insights.quote: we're not as humans going to be able to keep up with the pace of change that's going to come but because these things are coding and testing their successors.

insights.claim: Muddy Waters is positioning for AI-driven economic disruption through a capped-risk 'AI disruption basket': equity put spreads plus long credit spreads expressed via swaptions, rather than outright short positions, because a structural bid continues to suppress volatility and keep spreads tight.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete, replicable framework for expressing a bearish AI-disruption macro view while capping downside in a market where volatility remains structurally suppressed.
insights.quote: So, we do have an AI disruption basket of trades. We are in equities and credit, but in equities, it's put spreads.

insights.claim: Block had expected by February 2026 that markets would price in AI-driven disruption risk faster than they priced the 2008 financial crisis (which took roughly two years from first warning signs to full realization), but the Iran war distracted markets and delayed that repricing; he still expects a faster-than-GFC repricing once attention returns.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A timing signal -- watch for markets refocusing on AI disruption risk once other macro distractions fade.
insights.quote: I've suspected in February that the market was going to price this in at a much faster rate than the GFC was priced in.

insights.claim: As second- and third-order trades on the AI disruption thesis, Muddy Waters holds put spreads on corporate bond ETFs LQD and HYG, and is short the municipal bond ETF MUB, betting that fiscally stressed states like California, New York, Connecticut, New Jersey and Illinois -- the largest constituents of muni bond indices -- will trigger an ETF-versus-underlying liquidity mismatch that freezes redemptions before central banks intervene.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A specific watchlist of instruments for trading a credit-stress dislocation, with an explicit reminder to cover into the dislocation before a central-bank rescue arrives.
insights.quote: there will be a moment in time, right? If you're short the ETFs there, where there's this dislocation because the ETFs won't be able to meet the liquidity of the underlying.

insights.claim: Corporate and high-yield credit spreads are near their tightest levels ever (around 1%) even as few investors express concern, which the host and guest both find strange given the AI-disruption thesis.
insights.speaker: host (Meb Faber)
insights.so_what: Tight spreads despite a plausible disruption catalyst suggest credit protection may be cheap relative to the risk being discussed.
insights.quote: These are some of the tightest spreads ever. I mean, it's like 1%. Nobody seems bothered by that.

insights.claim: Two generations of investors have entered markets since the 2008 financial crisis having only learned that risk means 'buy the dip' because the Fed reliably rescues markets, which Block says explains both suppressed volatility and spreads and the near-disappearance of short sellers, since identifying risk hasn't been profitable.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A structural explanation for market complacency that could break if a shock arrives that the Fed can't or won't offset quickly.
insights.quote: I think there two generations of investors who've entered the market since the GFC. And to them, what is risk? It's a BTFD, right?

insights.claim: SoFi has used 'fair value option' accounting to mark newly originated personal loans up to roughly 108-109% of face value on day one -- far more aggressive than typical conservative loss provisioning -- making it, per Muddy Waters, the only bank Block could identify with over $20 billion in assets using this method for substantially all of its loans.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Flags an accounting practice that inflates reported income relative to peers, a red flag for earnings quality worth scrutinizing in filings.
insights.quote: SoFi's day one marks take it up the are look to be about 108, 109.

insights.claim: Muddy Waters alleges SoFi supported its aggressive day-one loan marks by selling loans to buyers at roughly 106% of face value while itself financing 80-90% of the purchase price at about 5%, well below the roughly 13% yield on the underlying loans, without disclosing that it was effectively subsidizing its own buyers.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: If accurate, the loan sales used to validate SoFi's marks aren't arm's-length transactions, undermining the legitimacy of reported gains.
insights.quote: it looks to be lending 80 to 90% of the purchase consideration, you know, we found this at about 5% and these are on loans that are yielding 13%

insights.claim: After the SEC sent SoFi a comment letter in September 2024 questioning disclosure around its 'secured lending program,' SoFi reported a $312 million 'loan sale' within about two weeks that Muddy Waters says was actually a transfer to a still-consolidated subsidiary financed by a JPMorgan loan, not a genuine sale, because the entity still appears on SoFi's list of significant consolidated subsidiaries.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A concrete, checkable disclosure discrepancy that investors or regulators could scrutinize to test the short thesis.
insights.quote: there was a $312 million what SoFi characterizes loan sale in that same quarter within a few days, within two weeks of receiving the comment letter

insights.claim: Muddy Waters estimates that if regulators disallow the accounting treatment behind that $312 million transaction, it would force SoFi to restate roughly $1 billion of previously reported EBITDA, since that transaction underpins the marks supporting a much larger base of reported gains.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Quantifies the potential earnings hit if the accounting thesis is validated -- a specific number to watch for in any SEC action or restatement.
insights.quote: our view is if this were disallowed, it pulling that linchpin would force a restatement of like a billion dollars of previously reported EBITDA.

insights.claim: SoFi's CEO and CFO have taken over $50 million off the table combined via forward sale agreements on their stock while technically still holding the shares, allowing them to claim they haven't sold any stock.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A governance red flag: insiders realizing liquidity from a stock whose reported earnings quality is being publicly challenged.
insights.quote: They entered into some forward agreement where they were able to take over $50 million off the table.

insights.claim: Block still considers China 'uninvestable' -- not as a directional index call but because the Ministry of State Security has forcibly removed journalists, researchers, and investigators who used to supply reliable information about Chinese companies, leaving investors with structurally poor-quality information.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A standing caution for allocators to steeply discount due-diligence claims about Chinese equities given the removal of independent information sources.
insights.quote: I still call it uninvestable and that's not a prediction that the blah blah blah index goes down by whatever.

insights.claim: Chinese regulatory policy is capricious enough that entire industries can be effectively destroyed overnight when officials decide they dislike a business, adding an unhedgeable tail risk on top of already poor information quality.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Reinforces avoiding long China exposure regardless of valuation, since policy risk can wipe out equity value without warning.
insights.quote: some of these Chinese names that I forget what industry, but just got like effectively bagled because the Ministry of whatever decided that they didn't like their business.

insights.claim: Block says foreign investors in Chinese VIE (variable interest entity) structures don't actually own the underlying operating businesses, and in his experience every Chinese operating company he's examined has been in material default of its contractual obligations to transfer money up through the VIE structure since day one.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Directly challenges the legal basis of foreign ownership claims in US-listed Chinese ADRs structured via VIEs, a systemic risk for anyone holding them.
insights.quote: they've all been in material default of these what we call VIE packages since day one.

insights.claim: The Wirecard fraud persisted for over a decade, from the earliest public critics in 2008 to its 2020 collapse, with German regulator BaFin taking the unprecedented step of suspending short-selling in Wirecard's stock following a leak about a critical 2019 Financial Times investigation -- the first time that crisis-era tool had been used to protect a single company.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Illustrates how long a well-evidenced fraud can survive with regulatory and political cover, a caution against assuming exposed frauds collapse quickly.
insights.quote: BaFin intervened and suspended short-selling on Wirecard stock, which was the first time since that framework had been created in Europe post-GFC.

insights.claim: Wirecard's former COO Jan Marsalek allegedly tipped off hedge fund managers in London ahead of the critical 2019 Financial Times report, and was later revealed to have been an intelligence asset working with Russia's GRU.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A reminder that egregious frauds can be entangled with state-level actors, raising the real-world risk profile of activist short campaigns against them.
insights.quote: it came out afterward that like, in all seriousness, this guy Jan Marsalek had been a GRU asset or agent.

insights.claim: Muddy Waters' short of French hypermarket operator Groupe Casino Guichard-Perrachon warned about debt hidden through largely legal accounting means; the company later collapsed under that debt load despite a hostile French government pursuing Muddy Waters for years, and the controlling shareholder was separately convicted of market manipulation.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Shows a well-researched short thesis can take years and face state-level hostility before vindication, relevant to sizing time horizons on activist shorts.

insights.claim: Muddy Waters' 2016 short of Japanese company Nidec, which flagged governance issues tied to its chairman, was vindicated years later when Nidec's own internal investigation confirmed the allegations and the chairman was removed.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: A validated long-cycle example that activist short research can eventually force real corporate governance change even in markets historically less receptive to shareholder activism.
insights.quote: They did an internal investigation. I mean ratified pretty much everything that we had said back in 2016.

themes.theme: Muddy Waters' New Bets: Momentum and Junior Mining

insights.claim: Muddy Waters launched a systematic momentum strategy within the S&P 500 in October 2024 that has compounded at over 70% gross returns, part of a deliberate diversification away from pure short activism.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Signals that even a dedicated short seller is shifting capital toward systematic long/momentum strategies given the poor risk-adjusted returns of shorting in this cycle.
insights.quote: it's compounded it over I think at over 70% 70 gross.

insights.claim: Muddy Waters has moved into junior mining, mostly on the long side including some activist campaigns (e.g., Snowline), on the thesis that the sector has suffered chronic under-allocation of investment talent since roughly 2000, creating venture-like return potential but with more available data than typical venture investing.
insights.speaker: Carson Block, guest
insights.so_what: Identifies a talent-starved corner of markets where a research-intensive investor could find outsized long-side edge.
insights.quote: there's been a huge under-allocation of talent to the mining space, you know, since at least 2000.
